title: Nyāyakusumāñjali: Stavaka 1
author: Udayana (Udayanācārya)
summary: The Nyāyakusumāñjali ('Offering of a Handful of Flowers of Reasoning') is Udayana's systematic rational defense of theism (īśvaravāda), built in five chapters (stavakas) of kārikās with the author's own prose commentary. Stavaka 1 opens the work by refuting the Mīmāṃsaka and Cārvāka positions that deny the need for a God, defending the reality of adṛṣṭa (unseen moral causation) and the dependence of the moral order on a supreme cause, thereby establishing the necessity of a regulating intelligent agent.
history: Udayana, a Maithila logician at the transition from the older Nyāya to Navya-Nyāya, composed the Nyāyakusumāñjali as the most elaborate Nyāya-Vaiśeṣika treatise on the existence of God; it codifies the classic arguments and attracted numerous later commentaries (e.g. by Varadarāja, Vardhamāna, and Haridāsa). It remains the foundational theistic text of the school.

title: Nyāyakusumāñjali: Stavaka 2
author: Udayana (Udayanācārya)
summary: Second chapter of Udayana's five-chapter theistic treatise. Stavaka 2 argues against those who hold that the world has no need of a cause, defending the principle that effects require an agent-cause and that the cosmos as a contingent product points beyond itself; it counters skeptical and non-theistic objections to causal reasoning that would undermine the inference to God.
history: Part of Udayana's Nyāyakusumāñjali, the school's classic demonstration of īśvara; the work's argumentative architecture culminates only in the final chapter, with the earlier stavakas dismantling rival anti-theistic positions one by one. Widely commented upon in later Nyāya scholasticism.

title: Nyāyakusumāñjali: Stavaka 3
author: Udayana (Udayanācārya)
summary: Third chapter of the Nyāyakusumāñjali. Stavaka 3 defends the existence of an enduring unseen agency (adṛṣṭa/dharma-adharma) and the soul as moral substrate, refuting the Buddhist doctrine of momentariness and the denial of a permanent self, since the theistic argument requires real agents whose merit and demerit must be supervised by a divine dispenser of results.
history: Within Udayana's anti-Buddhist and pro-theistic program, this chapter overlaps thematically with his Ātmatattvaviveka in rebutting Buddhist denial of permanent entities. The Nyāyakusumāñjali stands as Udayana's mature synthesis and was central to subsequent Navya-Nyāya theism.

title: Nyāyakusumāñjali: Stavaka 4
author: Udayana (Udayanācārya)
summary: Fourth chapter of the Nyāyakusumāñjali. Stavaka 4 takes up the validity of inference and verbal testimony as instruments by which God can be established, defending the means of knowledge (pramāṇa) against skeptical attacks and preparing the logical ground for the formal proof that follows.
history: This chapter secures the epistemological prerequisites of Udayana's theistic argument, reflecting his broader concern with vindicating the Nyāya pramāṇas. The treatise as a whole is the most fundamental Nyāya-Vaiśeṣika work on the īśvara doctrine.

title: Nyāyakusumāñjali: Stavaka 5
author: Udayana (Udayanācārya)
summary: The culminating fifth chapter of the Nyāyakusumāñjali, where Udayana presents his positive demonstration of the existence of God, marshalling a famous set of arguments (commonly counted as around nine) including the cosmological argument from effects (kāryāt), from the support of the world (dhṛti), and from the authority of scripture and the necessity of a guarantor of the moral law. Here the entire treatise's argumentative scaffolding is brought to bear on proving a single creator-Lord.
history: Stavaka 5 contains the school's classic proofs of God and is the most cited portion of the work; Udayana's formulation became the standard reference point that later thinkers such as Gaṅgeśa and his critics (e.g. Vyāsatīrtha) engaged with for centuries.

title: Vidhivāda
author: Gaṅgeśa (Gaṅgeśa Upādhyāya)
genre: philosophical treatise (section of a larger work)
date_estimate: first half of the 14th century CE (Gaṅgeśa fl. c. 1325)
summary: The Vidhivāda ('Discourse on Injunctions') is a section of the Śabdakhaṇḍa (the book on verbal testimony) of Gaṅgeśa's Tattvacintāmaṇi. It treats the philosophy of Vedic and other injunctions (vidhi): what an injunctive verbal form signifies, the nature of the impelling force (bhāvanā/pravartanā) it conveys, and how the listener derives motivation to act, engaging closely with Mīmāṃsā theories of command.
history: Gaṅgeśa of Mithilā founded the Navya-Nyāya ('New Logic') school, and his Tattvacintāmaṇi became the basic text for all later developments; the Vidhivāda within its Śabda book is a much-studied unit on the semantics of injunctions, treated in modern scholarship (e.g. V. N. Jha's edition with Śaśadhara's gloss).

title: Nyāyapariśuddhi: Bhūmikā
author: Veṅkaṭanātha (Vedānta Deśika)
summary: The Nyāyapariśuddhi ('Purification of Logic') is Vedānta Deśika's systematic treatise on the epistemology (pramāṇa theory) of Viśiṣṭādvaita Vedānta. The Bhūmikā is its introductory portion, setting out the scope of the work, the enumeration of the valid means of knowledge admitted by the school (perception, inference, and verbal testimony), and the polemical orientation that adapts and modifies Nyāya doctrine to suit Viśiṣṭādvaita.
history: Veṅkaṭanātha, the great Śrīvaiṣṇava poet-philosopher of Kāñcī/Śrīraṅgam, wrote the Nyāyapariśuddhi as the school's counterpart to Nyāya and Mīmāṃsā epistemology; it is regarded as a definitive introduction to Viśiṣṭādvaita pramāṇa theory and was followed by his own Nyāyasiddhāñjana and commentaries by his successors.

title: Nyāyapariśuddhi: Adhyāya 1.1
author: Veṅkaṭanātha (Vedānta Deśika)
summary: Opening section (chapter 1, part 1) of Vedānta Deśika's Nyāyapariśuddhi. This part establishes the general framework of pramāṇa theory, the definition and number of valid means of knowledge accepted in Viśiṣṭādvaita, and begins the detailed treatment of perception (pratyakṣa) as a source of valid cognition.
history: Part of the school's foundational epistemological treatise, in which Deśika adopts and modifies the Naiyāyika theory of pramāṇa to make it consistent with Rāmānuja's Viśiṣṭādvaita. Transmitted with traditional Śrīvaiṣṇava commentaries.

title: Nyāyapariśuddhi: Adhyāya 1.2
author: Veṅkaṭanātha (Vedānta Deśika)
summary: Chapter 1, part 2 of the Nyāyapariśuddhi, continuing the analysis of perception (pratyakṣa) and related epistemic issues within the Viśiṣṭādvaita scheme of valid cognition, including the conditions of valid perceptual knowledge and the refutation of rival accounts.
history: Section of Vedānta Deśika's authoritative Viśiṣṭādvaita epistemology, engaging Nyāya, Mīmāṃsā and Buddhist epistemologists while defending the Śrīvaiṣṇava position.

title: Nyāyapariśuddhi: Adhyāya 3.1
author: Veṅkaṭanātha (Vedānta Deśika)
summary: Chapter 3, part 1 of the Nyāyapariśuddhi. The third book turns to inference (anumāna): the structure of valid inferential reasoning, the nature of invariable concomitance (vyāpti), the members of a syllogism, and the fallacies, all reworked to fit Viśiṣṭādvaita commitments.
history: Continuation of Deśika's systematic pramāṇa exposition; his treatment of inference adapts Navya-Nyāya logical apparatus to Śrīvaiṣṇava theology.

title: Nyāyapariśuddhi: Adhyāya 3.2
author: Veṅkaṭanātha (Vedānta Deśika)
summary: Chapter 3, part 2 of the Nyāyapariśuddhi, continuing the treatment of inference (anumāna), including further analysis of the grounds of inferential validity, types of inference, and the refutation of opposing logical doctrines.
history: Section of Vedānta Deśika's epistemological treatise on the logic of inference within Viśiṣṭādvaita.

title: Nyāyapariśuddhi: Adhyāya 4.1
author: Veṅkaṭanātha (Vedānta Deśika)
summary: Chapter 4, part 1 of the Nyāyapariśuddhi. The fourth book deals with verbal testimony (śabda/āgama) as a means of valid knowledge: the authority of the Veda, how words convey meaning, sentence-meaning, and the privileged epistemic status of scripture for establishing supersensible truths in Viśiṣṭādvaita.
history: Because śabda is the decisive pramāṇa for Vedāntic theology, this book is central to Deśika's defense of scriptural authority against Mīmāṃsā and others.

title: Nyāyapariśuddhi: Adhyāya 4.2
author: Veṅkaṭanātha (Vedānta Deśika)
summary: Chapter 4, part 2 of the Nyāyapariśuddhi, continuing the treatment of verbal testimony (śabda): the apprehension of sentence-meaning, the role of expectancy, proximity and compatibility, and the validity of Vedic and authoritative human speech within the Viśiṣṭādvaita pramāṇa scheme.
history: Section of Deśika's exposition of śabda-pramāṇa, vindicating the epistemic authority of scripture and language for Śrīvaiṣṇava Vedānta.

title: Nyāyapariśuddhi: Adhyāya 5.1
author: Veṅkaṭanātha (Vedānta Deśika)
summary: Chapter 5, part 1 of the Nyāyapariśuddhi. The closing book addresses ancillary epistemological topics and the consolidation of the school's pramāṇa doctrine, including the reduction of disputed alleged means of knowledge (such as comparison, presumption, and non-apprehension) to the three accepted pramāṇas.
history: Concluding portion of Deśika's authoritative Viśiṣṭādvaita epistemology, settling the school's final position against rival enumerations of the pramāṇas.

title: Nyāyapariśuddhi: Adhyāya 5.2
author: Veṅkaṭanātha (Vedānta Deśika)
summary: Chapter 5, part 2, the final section of the Nyāyapariśuddhi, completing the treatment of disputed and reducible means of knowledge and bringing the work's systematic defense of the three-pramāṇa epistemology of Viśiṣṭādvaita to a close.
history: Closing section of Vedānta Deśika's epistemological masterwork, foundational for later Śrīvaiṣṇava logic and apologetics.

title: Vyomavatī
author: Vyomaśiva (Vyomaśivācārya)
date_estimate: c. 9th-10th century CE (commonly c. 900-1000 CE; some propose 8th c.)
summary: The Vyomavatī is a major commentary on Praśastapāda's Padārthadharmasaṅgraha, the standard manual of Vaiśeṣika categories (padārtha). It expounds and defends the Vaiśeṣika ontology of substance, quality, action, universal, particularity and inherence, treating at length issues of cognition, the means of knowledge, and the existence of God, and is one of the earliest surviving commentaries on Praśastapāda.
history: Vyomaśiva, a Śaiva ascetic-scholar associated with Kashmir, is generally regarded as the earliest of the three classic commentators on the Padārthadharmasaṅgraha (alongside Śrīdhara's Nyāyakandalī and Udayana's Kiraṇāvalī). His date is debated, ranging from the 8th to the 10th century, with most scholars favoring around 900-1000 CE.

title: Tarkatāṇḍava: Pariccheda 1
author: Vyāsatīrtha (Vyāsarāja)
tradition: Hindu (Dvaita / Mādhva Vedānta, polemicizing against Nyāya-Vaiśeṣika)
summary: The Tarkatāṇḍava ('Dance of Reason/Logic') is Vyāsatīrtha's exhaustive Dvaita critique of the Nyāya-Vaiśeṣika system, deploying the rigorous techniques of Navya-Nyāya against its own home tradition. Pariccheda 1 corresponds to the examination of perception (pratyakṣa), scrutinizing the Naiyāyika account of perceptual cognition and exposing its alleged inconsistencies from the Mādhva standpoint.
history: Vyāsatīrtha, the great Mādhva scholar-saint of the Vijayanagara court, composed the Tarkatāṇḍava as one of his three polemical masterworks (the Vyāsatraya, with the Nyāyāmṛta and Tātparyacandrikā). It engages Gaṅgeśa's Tattvacintāmaṇi closely, including the inference of God's existence, and shaped early-modern debates with Navya-Naiyāyikas.

title: Tarkatāṇḍava: Pariccheda 2
author: Vyāsatīrtha (Vyāsarāja)
tradition: Hindu (Dvaita / Mādhva Vedānta, polemicizing against Nyāya-Vaiśeṣika)
summary: Second section of the Tarkatāṇḍava. Following the structure of Nyāya pramāṇa theory, this pariccheda takes up inference (anumāna), critically dissecting the Naiyāyika doctrine of invariable concomitance and inferential reasoning, including Vyāsatīrtha's much-discussed treatment of Gaṅgeśa's inference for the existence of God.
history: Part of Vyāsatīrtha's encyclopaedic refutation of Nyāya-Vaiśeṣika logic; his critique here prompted later responses, notably Appaya Dīkṣita's revision of Gaṅgeśa's īśvarānumāna.

title: Tarkatāṇḍava: Pariccheda 3
author: Vyāsatīrtha (Vyāsarāja)
tradition: Hindu (Dvaita / Mādhva Vedānta, polemicizing against Nyāya-Vaiśeṣika)
summary: Third section of the Tarkatāṇḍava, corresponding to verbal testimony (śabda) and related epistemological topics. Here Vyāsatīrtha critiques the Nyāya theory of the meaning and authority of words and the school's account of valid linguistic cognition, defending the Mādhva position on scripture and language.
history: Concludes the three-part doxographical critique of Nyāya-Vaiśeṣika that, together with the Nyāyāmṛta and Tātparyacandrikā, constitutes Vyāsatīrtha's Vyāsatraya and influenced subsequent Dvaita-Nyāya polemics.

title: Īśvarapratyabhijñāvimarśinī
author: Abhinavagupta
summary: The Īśvarapratyabhijñāvimarśinī is Abhinavagupta's commentary (the shorter 'vimarśinī', as opposed to his longer Vivṛtivimarśinī) on Utpaladeva's Īśvarapratyabhijñākārikā, the foundational verse-text of the Pratyabhijñā ('Recognition') school of Kashmir Śaivism. It expounds the doctrine that liberation comes through recognizing one's own self as identical with the supreme Lord Śiva, developing a non-dual idealist metaphysics centered on consciousness (cit), reflective awareness (vimarśa), and divine freedom (svātantrya).
history: Abhinavagupta, the towering polymath of Kashmir Śaivism, wrote this commentary to make Utpaladeva's compressed kārikās accessible; it became, with the Vivṛtivimarśinī, the principal vehicle for transmitting Pratyabhijñā philosophy. The work was itself further commented upon (notably by Bhāskarakaṇṭha's Bhāskarī).
